narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 36. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 63.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower Year 3 school grade decile of 1, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a failure, well below the typical student's mid-range performance. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed somewhat in that direction, again lower than average. On a positive note, your LSAT score, close to the dataset average of 36.7, strongly pulled the prediction back toward success, as did your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is slightly below average but still helpful. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority of successful applicants and offered a small boost, while your middle family income bracket had minimal impact. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your grades in later years were notably weaker, tipping the scales despite your other solid metrics. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your profile stacked up against patterns in the data. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your grade deciles, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring for upper-year performance, and reapplying once you've strengthened those areas. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key predictors head-on. We're rooting for you to turn this around and join the ranks of bar passers. Keep pushing forward.
